clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of irregular periods occurring every 40–60 days and progressive facial hirsutism. Her BMI is 29 kg/m². Examination reveals acne on the face and upper back. Pelvic ultrasound shows bilateral ovaries each with 14 small follicles arranged peripherally and an ovarian volume of 12 mL. Serum LH is elevated at 12 IU/L and FSH is 4 IU/L (LH:FSH ratio 3:1). Free testosterone is mildly raised. Which combination of findings meets the minimum diagnostic threshold for polycystic ovary syndrome using the Rotterdam criteria?
options: Polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound alone
Oligo-anovulation plus polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound
explanation: Rotterdam criteria require at least 2 of 3 features: oligo- or anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology (PCOM). This patient has oligo-anovulation (cycles 40–60 days) and PCOM (≥12 follicles per ovary and/or volume >10 mL), satisfying the minimum of 2 criteria. Neither LH:FSH ratio nor obesity is part of the formal diagnostic criteria.
wrongAnswerExplanations: LH:FSH ratio is a supportive finding but is not one of the three Rotterdam diagnostic criteria and cannot diagnose PCOS alone.
PCOM on ultrasound is only one of the three Rotterdam features; at least two features are required for diagnosis.
Correct — oligo-anovulation and PCOM together satisfy 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria, which is the minimum required for diagnosis.
Hyperandrogenism is one valid criterion, but elevated LH alone is not a criterion; this combination does not form a valid Rotterdam pair.
Obesity and irregular menses are associated features but obesity is not a Rotterdam criterion; this combination is insufficient for diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: PCOS requires 2 of 3 Rotterdam features: oligo-anovulation, hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical), and PCOM on ultrasound.
keyClue: Cycles of 40–60 days (oligo-anovulation) combined with bilateral peripheral follicles and ovarian volume >10 mL (PCOM).
commonExamTrap: Candidates incorrectly rely on LH:FSH ratio as a diagnostic criterion — it is supportive, not part of Rotterdam.
missedConcept: Rotterdam criteria require only 2 of 3 features; LH:FSH and obesity are not among the three.

clinicalStem: A 27-year-old woman with suspected PCOS based on irregular periods and clinical hirsutism is referred for biochemical evaluation. Her cycles occur every 45–70 days. She has no galactorrhoea, no heat or cold intolerance, and no features of Cushing syndrome. BMI is 28 kg/m². Pelvic ultrasound confirms polycystic ovarian morphology. To confirm the diagnosis and exclude important differentials before labelling her with PCOS, which SINGLE investigation is the most essential first step?
options: Serum FSH and LH on day 2 of cycle
Serum free and total testosterone with sex hormone-binding globulin
Fasting serum cortisol and 24-hour urinary free cortisol
explanation: Before diagnosing PCOS, thyroid dysfunction and hyperprolactinaemia must be excluded as they both cause oligo-anovulation and menstrual irregularity mimicking PCOS. TSH and prolactin are the most essential exclusionary tests; guidelines recommend ruling these out as a prerequisite. AMH and androgen levels add to characterisation but do not exclude the primary mimics.
wrongAnswerExplanations: LH and FSH help characterise PCOS (high LH:FSH) but do not exclude the key differential diagnoses of thyroid disease and hyperprolactinaemia.
Testosterone and SHBG quantify hyperandrogenism but do not exclude thyroid disease or hyperprolactinaemia, which are the mandated exclusions before diagnosing PCOS.
Cortisol studies are indicated only when Cushing features are present; the stem explicitly states no Cushingoid features.
Correct — TSH and prolactin are the mandated exclusionary investigations; thyroid dysfunction and hyperprolactinaemia must be ruled out before diagnosing PCOS.
AMH is elevated in PCOS and useful for characterising ovarian reserve, but it is not an exclusionary test and not the first-line investigation.
clinicalPearl: Exclude hypothyroidism and hyperprolactinaemia (TSH + prolactin) before diagnosing PCOS — both mimic oligo-anovulation.
keyClue: The instruction to 'exclude important differentials' before labelling PCOS points directly to TSH and prolactin.
commonExamTrap: Choosing testosterone/SHBG or LH:FSH as the 'most essential first step' — these characterise PCOS but do not exclude the mandatory differential diagnoses.
missedConcept: Guidelines require exclusion of thyroid disease and hyperprolactinaemia before a PCOS diagnosis is assigned.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman with confirmed PCOS presents to the endocrine-gynaecology clinic. Her BMI is 34 kg/m², waist circumference is 96 cm, and she has acanthosis nigricans on the nape of her neck. Her periods occur every 50–90 days. She has no current complaint of thirst or polyuria. Random blood glucose taken at the visit is 7.2 mmol/L. Which investigation is the MOST appropriate next step to evaluate her metabolic status?
explanation: In obese women with PCOS and acanthosis nigricans (a sign of insulin resistance), a 75 g OGTT is the preferred investigation to detect impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) and type 2 diabetes. A random glucose of 7.2 mmol/L is not diagnostic of diabetes but is above the normal range, warranting formal glucose tolerance testing. OGTT detects IGT (2-hour glucose 7.8–11.0 mmol/L) that HbA1c can miss in early disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Fasting insulin is useful for calculating HOMA-IR as a research index of insulin resistance, but it is not a standardised clinical diagnostic test for glucose tolerance status.
HbA1c may miss early impaired glucose tolerance as it reflects average glucose over 3 months and has lower sensitivity than OGTT for detecting IGT in PCOS.
Correct — OGTT (75 g) is the gold-standard investigation to classify glucose tolerance in obese PCOS women and detects IGT that HbA1c may miss.
Fasting lipid profile is important for cardiovascular risk assessment but does not evaluate glucose metabolism, which is the primary concern here.
C-peptide measures endogenous insulin secretion and is used in the workup of hypoglycaemia or to distinguish type 1 from type 2 diabetes; it is not appropriate here.
clinicalPearl: Obese women with PCOS and acanthosis nigricans should have a 75 g OGTT to screen for impaired glucose tolerance and type 2 diabetes.
keyClue: Obesity, acanthosis nigricans, and a borderline random glucose (7.2 mmol/L) indicate insulin resistance requiring formal glucose tolerance testing.
commonExamTrap: Choosing HbA1c as the metabolic screen — HbA1c has lower sensitivity than OGTT for early IGT in PCOS.
missedConcept: OGTT detects impaired glucose tolerance that HbA1c misses, making it the preferred metabolic screen in high-risk PCOS patients.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old woman presents with a single episode of painless vaginal bleeding 8 years after her last menstrual period. She is not on hormone replacement therapy. She has a BMI of 33 kg/m², type 2 diabetes, and hypertension. She is nulliparous. Pelvic examination is unremarkable. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an endometrial thickness of 9 mm. What is the most likely underlying diagnosis requiring further evaluation?
explanation: Postmenopausal bleeding with an endometrial thickness >4 mm is the threshold requiring histological evaluation, and 9 mm is significantly thickened. This patient has multiple risk factors for endometrial carcinoma: obesity (BMI 33), type 2 diabetes, nulliparity, and hypertension — all associated with unopposed oestrogen. Endometrial carcinoma is the must-not-miss diagnosis and the most likely pathology in this high-risk cluster.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Atrophic vaginitis is the most common overall cause of postmenopausal bleeding, but the significantly thickened endometrium (9 mm) cannot be attributed to atrophy and mandates histological exclusion of carcinoma.

An endometrial polyp can cause postmenopausal bleeding and produce focal thickening, but it is a benign diagnosis and the high-risk profile here (obesity, DM, nulliparity) makes carcinoma the most likely diagnosis requiring exclusion.
Correct — endometrial carcinoma is the most important diagnosis given the thickened endometrium (9 mm) and the cluster of risk factors (obesity, DM, nulliparity, hypertension).
Cervical carcinoma presents with postmenopausal bleeding but typically manifests on pelvic examination and would not cause endometrial thickening; pelvic examination is unremarkable here.
An oestrogen-secreting granulosa cell tumour could cause endometrial stimulation, but this is much rarer than primary endometrial carcinoma in this risk profile.
clinicalPearl: Postmenopausal bleeding plus endometrial thickness >4 mm demands histological sampling to exclude endometrial carcinoma — the threshold diagnosis in any postmenopausal bleeder.
keyClue: Endometrial thickness 9 mm combined with obesity, diabetes, and nulliparity in a postmenopausal woman.
commonExamTrap: Attributing all postmenopausal bleeding to atrophic vaginitis without noting the thickened endometrium.
missedConcept: Endometrial thickness >4 mm post-menopause requires tissue sampling regardless of symptom severity.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with two episodes of light vaginal bleeding over the past month. She had her last period at age 52. She is not on HRT. There is no pelvic pain. Speculum examination shows a normal-looking cervix. Transvaginal ultrasound reveals a homogeneous endometrial thickness of 11 mm. What is the MOST appropriate next investigation?
explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line investigation for postmenopausal bleeding; when endometrial thickness exceeds 4–5 mm, histological sampling is mandatory. Outpatient endometrial biopsy (pipelle sampling) is the next step — it is minimally invasive, has high sensitivity for detecting endometrial carcinoma (>90%), and avoids anaesthetic risk. MRI is useful for staging once carcinoma is confirmed, not for primary diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI is the investigation of choice for staging confirmed endometrial carcinoma (myometrial invasion, lymph node assessment) but is not the next step for diagnosis when histology has not yet been obtained.
CA-125 is neither sensitive nor specific enough for primary diagnosis of endometrial carcinoma; it may be elevated in advanced disease but cannot substitute for tissue sampling.
Correct — outpatient endometrial biopsy (pipelle) is the mandatory next step when TVU shows endometrial thickness >4–5 mm in a postmenopausal bleeder.
Diagnostic laparoscopy has no role in the investigation of postmenopausal bleeding; it does not sample the endometrium.
Repeating the ultrasound in 6 weeks is inappropriate — the endometrium is already clearly thickened (11 mm) and tissue sampling cannot be deferred.
clinicalPearl: When TVU shows endometrial thickness >4–5 mm in a postmenopausal bleeder, immediate outpatient endometrial biopsy is the next investigation — do not defer.
keyClue: Endometrial thickness 11 mm on TVU in a postmenopausal woman with bleeding.
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI before obtaining histology — MRI stages disease, it does not diagnose it.
missedConcept: TVU screens; endometrial biopsy diagnoses. The sequence is TVU → biopsy when thickness >4–5 mm.

clinicalStem: A 66-year-old woman presents with spotting of blood on her underwear on three occasions over the past 2 months. She reports mild vaginal dryness and dyspareunia. She is not on HRT. On speculum examination the vaginal walls appear pale, thin, and easily traumatised. There is no cervical lesion. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an endometrial thickness of 3 mm. What is the most likely cause of her postmenopausal bleeding?
options: Granulosa cell tumour of the ovary
explanation: Atrophic vaginitis is the commonest cause of postmenopausal bleeding overall. The classic triad is thin pale vaginal epithelium, vaginal dryness/dyspareunia, and a reassuringly thin endometrium (<4 mm). With an endometrial thickness of only 3 mm, endometrial carcinoma is very unlikely and tissue sampling is not mandatory. The clinical and ultrasound picture is entirely consistent with urogenital atrophy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Endometrial carcinoma is excluded as a primary concern by the thin endometrium (3 mm, well below the 4–5 mm threshold); the clinical picture points to atrophy.
Cervical carcinoma would typically produce a visible lesion on speculum examination; none is described here.
An endometrial polyp would usually show focal endometrial thickening on ultrasound, not a uniformly thin endometrium of 3 mm.
Correct — atrophic vaginitis with its thin, easily traumatised vaginal walls, dyspareunia, and thin endometrium (3 mm) is the most likely diagnosis.
A granulosa cell tumour secretes oestrogen and would cause endometrial stimulation, producing a thickened endometrium, not a 3 mm thin one.
clinicalPearl: Atrophic vaginitis is the most common cause of postmenopausal bleeding; thin pale vaginal walls plus endometrial thickness <4 mm makes it the diagnosis.
keyClue: Thin, pale, easily traumatised vaginal walls with dyspareunia and endometrial thickness 3 mm.
commonExamTrap: Assuming all postmenopausal bleeding requires endometrial sampling — endometrial thickness <4 mm with atrophic vaginal changes points to atrophy.
missedConcept: An endometrial thickness <4 mm in a postmenopausal bleeder makes carcinoma very unlikely and shifts the diagnosis toward atrophic vaginitis.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old G3P3 woman delivers a 4.2 kg baby vaginally after a prolonged second stage of 2 hours and 20 minutes of active pushing. Active management of the third stage is performed with oxytocin; the placenta delivers intact 8 minutes later. Five minutes after placental delivery she is noted to have continuing heavy vaginal bleeding estimated at 800 mL. Her uterus is palpable at the level of the umbilicus and feels soft and boggy on abdominal examination. Vital signs: BP 98/62 mmHg, pulse 112 bpm. What is the MOST LIKELY cause of this primary PPH?
explanation: Uterine atony accounts for approximately 70–80% of all primary PPH cases. The classic presentation is a soft, boggy, poorly contracted uterus at or above the umbilicus after delivery. Risk factors include grand multiparity (G3P3), macrosomia (4.2 kg), and prolonged second stage — all of which lead to uterine muscle fatigue and failure to contract adequately.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Retained placental tissue causes PPH but the placenta has delivered intact in this case; this is therefore excluded as the primary cause.

Correct — uterine atony is the most common cause of PPH; a soft, boggy uterus after delivery of an intact placenta with risk factors (multiparity, macrosomia, prolonged labour) is the classic presentation.
Genital tract lacerations cause PPH with a well-contracted firm uterus; here the uterus is soft and boggy, indicating atony rather than a laceration.
DIC is a secondary coagulopathy that complicates massive PPH; it does not initiate bleeding and there are no described features of coagulopathy at this stage.
Uterine inversion is rare and presents with a palpable mass in the vagina, severe pain, and absence of a uterine fundus abdominally — none of which are described here.
clinicalPearl: A soft, boggy uterus after an intact placental delivery = uterine atony; it accounts for ~80% of PPH. Risk factors include multiparity, macrosomia, and prolonged labour.
keyClue: Soft, boggy uterus at the umbilicus with intact placenta delivery and risk factors of macrosomia and multiparity.
commonExamTrap: Selecting retained placenta — but the placenta delivered intact, ruling this out.
missedConcept: Uterine atony is the most common cause of PPH; a firm well-contracted uterus with ongoing bleeding redirects to lacerations or coagulopathy.
redFlagReason: Active PPH (>500 mL) with haemodynamic compromise (BP 98/62, HR 112) constitutes obstetric haemorrhagic shock requiring immediate identification and treatment of the cause.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman has delivered vaginally and is now estimated to have lost 1,200 mL of blood over 30 minutes. She is pale, cold, and clammy. BP is 82/50 mmHg, heart rate is 128 bpm, and oxygen saturation is 95% on room air. The uterus remains poorly contracted despite two doses of oxytocin and ergometrine. Two large-bore IV cannulae have been inserted. The attending team is managing the haemorrhage. Which set of investigations is MOST immediately relevant to guide ongoing resuscitation and identify life-threatening complications?
options: Full blood count, clotting screen (PT/APTT/fibrinogen), and cross-match
Urine culture, renal function, and serum electrolytes
Serum cortisol, blood cultures, and liver function tests
explanation: In massive PPH with haemodynamic compromise, the priority investigations are FBC (haemoglobin, platelet count), coagulation screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen — to detect consumptive coagulopathy/DIC), and urgent cross-match for packed red cells. Fibrinogen falling below 2 g/L is an early predictor of severe PPH and DIC. These results directly guide transfusion decisions. Imaging is not the immediate priority during active resuscitation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Imaging (ultrasound or CT) is not the immediate priority during active haemorrhagic shock; resuscitation and identification of coagulopathy drive immediate clinical decisions.
Correct — FBC, clotting screen, and cross-match are the essential urgent investigations; they quantify anaemia, detect DIC, and enable blood product transfusion.
Renal function and electrolytes are secondary investigations; they do not guide immediate resuscitation decisions in active PPH.
Cultures and liver function tests are irrelevant to the immediate management of obstetric haemorrhagic shock.
ECG and CXR may be relevant in peri-arrest evaluation but are not the primary investigations driving resuscitation in PPH.
clinicalPearl: In massive PPH, urgent FBC + coagulation screen (fibrinogen is key) + cross-match guide transfusion and early detection of DIC.
keyClue: Haemodynamic compromise (BP 82/50, HR 128) with ongoing PPH after oxytocics — DIC must be actively sought with clotting studies.
commonExamTrap: Ordering imaging first in a haemodynamically unstable patient — clinical resuscitation and haematological workup take priority.
missedConcept: Fibrinogen <2 g/L is an early marker of PPH-associated DIC and indicates need for fibrinogen replacement (cryoprecipitate).
redFlagReason: Haemorrhagic shock (BP 82/50, HR 128) from massive PPH (1,200 mL in 30 min) is an obstetric emergency threatening maternal life from exsanguination and DIC.

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman who delivered vaginally 12 days ago presents to the emergency department with heavy vaginal bleeding and passage of dark clots since morning. She had an uneventful delivery with a blood loss of 350 mL. She has had mild lower abdominal cramps for the past 3 days and her lochia has not fully resolved. Temperature is 37.9°C, BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 94 bpm. On pelvic examination the uterus is slightly enlarged and tender, and the cervical os is open to the tip of the examining finger. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Primary PPH due to uterine atony
Secondary PPH due to retained products of conception
explanation: Secondary PPH is defined as abnormal bleeding from 24 hours to 12 weeks after delivery. The triad of subinvolution (enlarged tender uterus), open cervical os, and offensive/prolonged lochia strongly suggests retained products of conception (RPOC), which cause secondary PPH by preventing adequate uterine contraction and sometimes by becoming infected. The mild pyrexia may indicate concurrent endometritis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Primary PPH occurs within the first 24 hours of delivery; this episode at 12 days is by definition secondary PPH.
Correct — secondary PPH at 12 days with an open os, subinvolution, and ongoing lochia is characteristic of retained products of conception.

Endometritis causes fever, uterine tenderness, and offensive lochia but is usually not associated with an open cervical os or heavy clot passage; the haemorrhage here suggests RPOC rather than isolated infection.
Ovarian cyst rupture causes acute pelvic pain with signs of intraperitoneal haemorrhage (peritonism, haemoperitoneum) rather than vaginal bleeding through an open os.
Cervical ectropion causes light contact bleeding (typically spotting), not heavy bleeding with clots and a tender subinvoluted uterus.
clinicalPearl: Secondary PPH (24h–12 weeks post-delivery) with an open cervical os, subinvolution, and prolonged lochia = retained products of conception until proven otherwise.
keyClue: Open cervical os, enlarged tender uterus, heavy bleeding with clots at 12 days postpartum.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing endometritis alone and missing the mechanical cause — RPOC prevents uterine involution and is the primary driver of secondary PPH.
missedConcept: Secondary PPH at 7–14 days postpartum with uterine subinvolution most commonly results from retained products of conception.

topic: PPH — best investigation for secondary PPH
clinicalStem: A 30-year-old woman presents 10 days after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery with heavy vaginal bleeding and clots. Her uterus is larger than expected on abdominal palpation. Her temperature is 37.5°C. A clinical diagnosis of secondary PPH with suspected retained products of conception is made. What is the MOST appropriate initial investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) is the first-line investigation for suspected retained products of conception. It identifies echogenic intrauterine material, measures endometrial thickness, and can detect subinvolution, vascularity (with Doppler), and haematometra. It is safe, readily available, and guides the decision between conservative management and surgical evacuation. Hysteroscopy and MRI are reserved for cases where ultrasound is inconclusive.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI provides excellent soft-tissue detail but is expensive, not immediately available in most settings, and unnecessary as a first-line tool when TVS can reliably diagnose RPOC.
Hysteroscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic for RPOC but is an invasive procedure requiring anaesthesia; TVS should be performed first to confirm the diagnosis.
Pelvic X-ray has no diagnostic value in the assessment of soft tissue uterine pathology such as RPOC.
Correct — TVS is the first-line imaging investigation for secondary PPH with suspected RPOC; it is safe, available, and directly guides management.
Serum beta-hCG may be weakly positive with RPOC but is non-specific; it cannot confirm or localise retained tissue and does not guide surgical decision-making.
clinicalPearl: Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line investigation for secondary PPH; echogenic intrauterine material with or without vascularity on Doppler confirms RPOC.
keyClue: Suspected retained products of conception — the question asks for initial, first-line investigation.
commonExamTrap: Proceeding directly to hysteroscopy before TVS — imaging must precede invasive procedures.
missedConcept: TVS is the standard first-line investigation for RPOC; hysteroscopy is reserved for inconclusive imaging or as a combined diagnostic-therapeutic procedure.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman underwent a total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for large symptomatic fibroids 6 days ago. She now presents with a temperature of 38.9°C, lower abdominal tenderness, and purulent discharge from the lower end of her midline incision. The wound edges are red, swollen, and there is a 2 cm area of dehiscence with seropurulent exudate. WBC count is 18,000/mm³. What is the MOST LIKELY cause of her post-operative fever?
explanation: Post-operative wound infection typically presents from day 5–7 onwards with local signs of erythema, swelling, purulent discharge, and wound dehiscence alongside systemic features of fever and leucocytosis. The '5 W's' of post-operative fever (Wind, Water, Wound, Walking/DVT, Wonder drugs) aid in temporal differential diagnosis: early fever (1–2 days) suggests atelectasis; day 3–5 suggests UTI; day 5–7 onwards suggests wound infection, deep infection, or DVT.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Atelectasis/pulmonary complications cause early post-operative fever (day 1–2) with respiratory symptoms; the fever here is on day 6 with clear local wound signs, making this incorrect.
UTI causes fever at day 3–5 with urinary symptoms (dysuria, frequency); no urinary symptoms are described and the local wound changes point to wound infection.
Correct — wound infection with erythema, purulent discharge, dehiscence, and leucocytosis on post-operative day 6 is the classic and most likely cause here.
DVT causes low-grade fever, calf tenderness, and leg swelling; no leg symptoms are described and the purulent wound discharge localises the source.
Drug fever is a diagnosis of exclusion with no localising signs; the obvious wound source makes this highly unlikely.
clinicalPearl: Post-operative wound infection classically presents on day 5–7 with local signs (erythema, pus, dehiscence) — the '5 W's' timeline helps differential diagnosis of post-op fever.
keyClue: Day 6 post-op fever with purulent wound discharge, erythema, and partial dehiscence.
commonExamTrap: Selecting UTI — no urinary symptoms are described, and the wound is the obvious local source of infection.
missedConcept: Using the 5 W's timeline: Wind (day 1–2), Water/UTI (day 3–5), Wound (day 5–7), Walking/DVT (day 5+), Wonder drugs (any time).

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman is on post-operative day 4 following a radical hysterectomy for cervical carcinoma. She suddenly develops pleuritic right-sided chest pain and breathlessness. She has a temperature of 37.8°C, RR 24/min, SpO2 91% on room air, BP 100/70 mmHg, and HR 118 bpm. Chest examination reveals reduced air entry at the right base. Her legs are oedematous and she has been on bed rest since surgery. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with S1Q3T3 pattern. Her pre-test probability for pulmonary embolism using the Wells score is 6 (high). Which SINGLE investigation will BEST confirm the diagnosis?
explanation: With a high pre-test probability (Wells score 6), D-dimer is not useful — a negative D-dimer does not rule out PE in high probability, and a positive one is non-specific. CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) is the definitive investigation of choice for suspected PE; it directly visualises thrombus in the pulmonary arterial tree, is widely available, fast, and provides an alternative diagnosis if PE is absent. V/Q scan is reserved for when CTPA is contraindicated (e.g., contrast allergy, renal failure).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Chest X-ray may show non-specific signs (atelectasis, Hampton's hump, Westermark sign) but is neither sensitive nor specific for PE; it cannot confirm the diagnosis.
D-dimer is useful only with low-to-moderate pre-test probability; with a high Wells score (6), a negative D-dimer does not exclude PE and a positive one is non-diagnostic.
V/Q scan is an alternative when CTPA is contraindicated; CTPA is preferred as the definitive investigation when renal function and contrast allergy are not a concern.
Correct — CTPA is the gold-standard confirmatory investigation for pulmonary embolism, directly visualising intraluminal thrombus with high sensitivity and specificity.
Lower limb Doppler ultrasound detects DVT (the source of PE) but does not confirm pulmonary embolism; a negative leg Doppler does not exclude PE.
clinicalPearl: For high pre-test probability PE, proceed directly to CTPA — D-dimer is unreliable when pre-test probability is high, as it is almost universally positive.
keyClue: High Wells score (6), post-surgical immobility, pleuritic chest pain, SpO2 91%, sinus tachycardia with S1Q3T3 — confirmed by CTPA.
commonExamTrap: Ordering D-dimer in a high-probability patient — D-dimer is used to EXCLUDE PE in low/moderate probability, not to confirm it.
missedConcept: D-dimer should not be ordered when pre-test probability is high; proceed directly to CTPA.
redFlagReason: Probable massive pulmonary embolism with haemodynamic compromise (BP 100/70, HR 118, SpO2 91%) represents an immediately life-threatening post-operative emergency.
